result:
I've been thinking about maps lately—not the GPS kind that bosses us around with mechanical certainty, but the old paper ones that used to live in glove compartments and get refolded wrong a thousand times.

There's something beautiful about how those maps acknowledged their own limitations. They had honest blank spaces marked "unexplored" or simply left white at the edges. The really old ones had sea monsters drawn in the margins, as if to say: "Here be dragons, and honestly, we have no idea what else."

Modern maps pretend to know everything. Every street, every building, updated in real-time by satellites that never blink. But they've lost something in their omniscience—the sense of mystery, the invitation to discover. When everything is mapped, nothing is truly explored.

I wonder if we're the same way with knowledge now. We have this illusion that Google holds all answers, that somewhere in the cloud exists a complete map of truth. But maybe the most interesting territories are still unmapped: the space between sleeping and waking, the exact color of loneliness, the reason certain songs can transport us instantly to a different decade.

Perhaps wisdom isn't about filling in all the blank spaces, but about learning to navigate comfortably in unmapped territory, to be okay with the dragons we haven't named yet, to fold and unfold our uncertainties until we know their creases by heart.

The best conversations happen in those unmapped places anyway.
